headline: Density Altitude Season is Here
body: Think you know Colorado density altitude? Think again. Even seasoned pilots get humbled on a warm Front Range afternoon. At 18V we're sitting at 4,934 ft MSL — add a 75°F day and you're performing at over 8,000 ft density altitude before you've left the pattern. Lean aggressively, rotate with patience, and give yourself the whole runway. We've got 4,100 ft of paved and 2,500 ft of grass. Use it.

headline: Grass Strip Etiquette — The Unwritten Rules
body: There's something about a grass strip that just gets in your blood. 14/32 at Platte Valley is 2,500 ft of soft-field character-building. A few things seasoned regulars know: inspect first, especially after rain. Soft-field technique isn't optional — it's respect for the surface. And if you're in a heavy nosedragger, let the taildraggers and Cubs go first. They earned it.

headline: Why Sunrise Departures Matter at 18V
body: There's a reason the regulars are out on the ramp by 6:30 AM. The air is smooth, the winds are calm, and the Rockies are lit up in alpenglow. Summer thermals at noon are a different conversation — but that early morning slot? That's what this field is really about. Come fly with us.

headline: AOPA: Small Airports Are the Backbone of GA
body: AOPA's latest advocacy push is highlighting what pilots at fields like 18V already know: small community airports are irreplaceable. They're where flight training happens, where first solos are celebrated, where the real GA culture lives. Platte Valley has been doing this for 60+ years — and we're not going anywhere.

headline: Mountain Wave Season — Respect the Rotor
body: Winter on the Front Range means mountain wave. If you're departing 18V westbound when the winds are howling off the peaks, know what you're getting into. The rotor zone below the wave can be violent. Stay east of the foothills until you have altitude, file IFR if in doubt, and never underestimate the lee side. Beautiful flying country — if you know the rules.
